4: ho ktisas ap arches arsen kai thely epoiesen autous — the one who created from the beginning made them male and female — Jesus grounds the marriage argument in the arche (beginning = creation order), which has theological priority over Mosaic legislation. The creation-given (ktisas, aorist passive) is the theological bedrock from which human institutional arrangements derive; Moses's concession accommodated fallen humanity but did not abrogate the creation norm. The two-become-one-flesh (Gen 2:24) is not a Mosaic law but a creational institution that precedes law.
1: metêren apò tês Galilaías — departed from Galilee — Metaírō (to lift up, move away) appears only in Matthew (11:1; 13:53; 19:1) as a structural marker for Jesus’ movements at discourse-end. Péran toû Iordánou = beyond the Jordan, the territory of Perea in Transjordan.
2: ēkoloúthēsan autô̧ óchloi polloí — many crowds followed him — The imperfect ēkoloúthēsan suggests continuous following as Jesus traveled. Etherápeusen (healed) aorist, summarizing healing activity without detailing individual cases.
3: katà pâsan aitian — for any reason — This phrase marks the Pharisaic test as a Hillelite/Shammaite debate question: Hillel permitted divorce for any displeasure (kol davar), Shammai restricted it to sexual immorality. Peirázōn (testing) marks their intent as adversarial. Ei éxestin = whether it is lawful (the standard Pharisaic form for halakhic inquiry).
5: kataleíptei patéra kaì mētéra — shall leave father and mother — Quotes Gen 2:24 LXX (proskollēthḗsetai = shall be joined/glued to, future passive). The verb proskolláō (to glue to, cleave) indicates the new primary bond surpasses even the parent-child bond.
6: hṑste oukéti eisìn dýo allà sàrx mía — so that they are no longer two but one flesh — Hōste marks consequence; sàrx mía (one flesh) is not merely metaphor but an ontological description. Ho theòs synézeuxen (God yoked together) uses syzeugnýō (to yoke, harness a pair together), the technical agricultural term for yoking two animals as one working unit.
7: dià tí oun Mōysês enetéilato doûnai biblíon apostasiou — why then did Moses command to give a certificate of divorce? — Enetéilato (commanded) vs. Jesus’ correction: the Pharisees read Deut 24:1 as a positive command; Jesus will reframe it as a concession. Biblíon apostasiou (LXX Deut 24:1) = document of separation.
8: Mōysês pròs tḕn sklērokardían hymôn — Moses with a view to your hardness of heart — Sklērokardía (hard-heartedness, from sklērós = hard + kardía = heart) is a compound biblical term (LXX Deut 10:16; Jer 4:4). The preposition prós (toward, in relation to) marks the divorce permission as accommodation to a condition, not endorsement. Ap' archês dè ou gégonen (but from the beginning it was not so) — the aorist perfect negation asserts that original design never included divorce.
9: mḕ epì porneía — except for sexual immorality — The porneia exception clause (also 5:32) is uniquely Matthean; Mark and Luke omit it. Porneía spans illicit sexual unions (premarital, adultery, incest) in Hellenistic usage. The clause’s precise scope has generated extensive patristic and Reformation debate; the Greek syntax attaches the exception only to the divorce, not to remarriage in all MSS.
10: ei oútōs estìn hē aitíà toû anthrṓpou metà tês gynaikós — if such is the case of a man with his wife — The disciples’ response reveals they operated with the Hillelite assumption: if divorce is nearly impossible, perhaps celibacy is preferable. Symphérei (it is advantageous) is utilitarian calculation.
11: ou pántes chōroûsin tòn lógon toûton — not all receive this word — Chōréō (to have room for, hold, receive) is metaphorical for intellectual/spiritual capacity. The lógon toûton (this word) refers to the demanding standard of marital permanence just stated, not to celibacy specifically. Hois dédotai (to whom it has been given) = divine passive, God-given capacity.
12: eunouchoí — eunuchs — Three categories: (1) born eunuchs (ek koilías mētrós), (2) made eunuchs by men, (3) those who eunouchísan heautoús (made themselves eunuchs) for the kingdom. The third category is clearly metaphorical — Origen’s literal self-castration was universally rejected as misreading. Ho dynámenos chōreîn chōreítō (let the one able to receive it receive it) echoes the selective call of v.11.
13: prosénechthēsan autô̧ paidía — children were brought to him — Prosféreō (to bring to, offer) is the verb for presenting offerings; used here of presenting children for blessing (epithȩ̂ tas cheîras = lay hands on and pray). The disciples’ rebuke (epetímēsan) reveals lingering hierarchy assumptions just overturned in ch18.
14: áphete tà paidía kaì mḕ kōlýete autà — let the children come and do not hinder them — Kōlýō (to hinder, prevent) is the technical baptism-refusal verb (Acts 8:36; 10:47); its use here suggests an early connection to the question of children’s participation in the covenant community. Tôn toioútōn estin hē basileía (of such ones is the kingdom) = genitive of possession or belonging, not merely comparison.
15: epitheis tas cheîras autoîs — having laid hands on them — Epitíthēmi cheîras is the laying-on-of-hands formula for blessing, ordination, and healing throughout the NT. Eporeúthē ekeîthen (departed from there) marks movement.
16: didáskale, tí agathòn poiḗsō — teacher, what good thing shall I do — The address didáskale (teacher) rather than kýrie (Lord) distinguishes this questioner’s orientation: he approaches Jesus as a moral expert, not as divine Lord. His question frames eternal life as achievable by a single act (poiḗsō, future deliberative).
17: tí me erōtâs perì toû agathoû — why do you ask me about the good? — Jesus redirects the question from his own person to God: heîs estin ho agathós (one is good). The question challenges the premise that goodness is a human achievement category; goodness is ontologically God’s alone. Then he answers pragmatically: têrei tàs entolás (keep the commandments).
18: poías — which ones? — The man’s follow-up question is not ignorant but probing: which subset of commandments constitutes the required minimum? Jesus’ response (tó: ou phoneúseis through agapḗseis tòn plēsíon) cites the Decalogue’s second table (duties to humans) plus Lev 19:18, grounding the social ethics in love-of-neighbor as summary.
19: timá tòn patéra kaì tḕn mētéra — honor your father and mother — Citing Ex 20:12 / Deut 5:16 LXX. The Decalogue items are listed in non-sequential order (murder, adultery, theft, false witness, honor parents); the neighbor-love summary from Lev 19:18 is added as a final encompassing principle.
20: pánta taûta ephylaxa — all these things I have kept — Phylássō (to guard, keep, observe) in aorist — he claims complete past observance. Tí éti hysterô̧ (what do I still lack) reveals his sense of insufficiency despite legal compliance, a remarkable admission that drives the encounter forward.
21: ei théleis téleos eînai — if you wish to be perfect — Téleios (perfect, complete, mature) in Matthew points to wholeness of undivided commitment (5:48). The command pṓlēsón sou tà hypárchonta (sell your possessions) is aorist imperative — immediate, total action. Thēsauròn en ouraî̧ (treasure in heaven) is the exchange offered. The barrier exposed is not intellectual but volitional: déuro akolouthei moi (come, follow me) — he won’t.
22: apêlthen lypoúmenos — he went away grieved — Lypoúmenos (present passive participle of lypéō) = being grieved, in pain. The grief itself indicates the man understood the invitation; he was not indifferent. Ên gàr échōn ktḗmata pollá (for he had many possessions) — the ên échōn periphrastic imperfect stresses his possession as a continuous state, not a temporary circumstance.
23: dyskolōs ploúsios eiseleúsetai — with difficulty a rich man will enter — Dýskolos (difficult, hard, troublesome) is an adverb; the placement is emphatic. The future indicative eiseleúsetai states a general truth about difficulty, not impossibility.
24: kámēlon dià trymaliâs rhaphis — a camel through the eye of a needle — Kámēlos (camel) is the large quadruped; a few MSS read kamilos (ship’s cable/rope) but the camel reading is overwhelmingly supported. No archaeological evidence supports the 'Needle Gate' tradition (a small Jerusalem gate) — this appears post-medieval. The image is intentionally absurd: maximum impossibility. Trymaliá rhaphis = needle-hole (rhaphis = sewing needle).
25: tís ára dýnatai sōthênai — who then is able to be saved? — The disciples’ shock (exeplḗssonto sphodra = were utterly amazed) shows they held the common assumption that wealth indicates divine favor (cf. Deut 28). If even the prosperous cannot be saved, who can? The question exposes the bankruptcy of merit-based soteriology.
26: parà anthrṓpois toûto adýnatón estin — with men this is impossible — Parà + dative (with, beside, from the perspective of). Parà dè theô̧ pánta dynatá (but with God all things are possible) — the contrast is absolute. Salvation is not a human achievement category at all; it is entirely divine gift.
27: hēmeîs aphḗkamen pánta — we left everything — Peter’s aorist aphḗkamen (left) echoes the exact word used for the rich man’s failure: he was unwilling to leave (aphés). Tí ára éstai hēmîn (what then will there be for us) — future deliberative question about reward.
28: en tȩ̂ palingenesiá — in the regeneration/renewal — Palingenesía (from pálin = again + génesis = birth/origin) appears only twice in NT: here and Tit 3:5. Here it refers to the cosmic renewal at the consummation, not individual rebirth. Hótan kathísē ho huiòs toû anthrṓpou epì thrónou dóxēs autoû — the enthroned Son of Man imagery from Dan 7. The Twelve will sit on twelve thrones judging the twelve tribes: eschatological co-regency.
29: pás hóstis aphêken oikías è adelphoús — everyone who has left houses or brothers — The catalog of forsaken relationships (family, property) is exhaustive. Hekatontaplasíona lḗmpsetai (will receive a hundredfold) is future indicative; zōḕn aiṓnion klēronomeî (will inherit eternal life) uses the inheritance idiom of covenant fulfillment.
30: polloi dè ésontai prôtoi éschatoi kaì éschatoi prôtoi — but many first will be last and last first — The future ésontai places this reversal in the eschatological order. The saying frames the parable that follows in ch20 as its illustration and serves as an inclusio: 19:30 and 20:16 bracket the parable with the same reversal-formula.
